item.title: Electoral Commission returns to full strength ahead of general election
item.description: The Electoral Commission has returned to its full complement following the appointment of two new Electoral Commissioners and the reappointment of three existing members as Fiji prepares for the next General Election. The Commission welcomed the appointments of Nicola Vuetilovoni and Raumanu Georgina Pranjivan, alongside the reappointment of Ratu Inoke Loganimoce, Nemani Mati and Reginald Jokhan t
item.content:encoded:
The Electoral Commission has returned to its full complement following the appointment of two new Electoral Commissioners and the reappointment of three existing members as Fiji prepares for the next General Election.

The Commission welcomed the appointments of Nicola Vuetilovoni and Raumanu Georgina Pranjivan, alongside the reappointment of Ratu Inoke Loganimoce, Nemani Mati and Reginald Jokhan to new terms.

Chairperson Justice Usaia Ratuvili formally welcomed the new and returning Commissioners during the Commission’s fifth meeting of 2026, saying the appointments strengthen the body’s ability to carry out its constitutional responsibilities.

“Our role is very clear under the Constitution and by statute. Collectively with the FEO, we are the Elections Management Body for Fiji. The General Elections will be carried out by the FEO, the Supervisor of Elections and the Secretary to the Commission,” Justice Ratuvili said.

He said restoring the Commission to its full membership was important as it prepares to undertake its supervisory and appellate functions during the electoral period.

Justice Ratuvili reaffirmed the Commission’s commitment to delivering credible, secure and transparent elections in partnership with the Fijian Elections Office.

Ms Vuetilovoni brings more than 30 years of experience in architecture, project management, property, tourism and corporate governance. She has held several governance roles, including serving as Chair of Habitat for Humanity Fiji, and is currently a director of Uluda Holdings Limited.

Ms Pranjivan is an experienced lawyer with expertise in public law, governance and commercial litigation. She previously served for more than 11 years in the Office of the Attorney-General before entering private practice and has represented Fiji internationally, including during the country’s COP23 Presidency.

The Electoral Commission said it looks forward to working with all Commissioners as preparations continue for the next General Election, with the shared objective of upholding the integrity, transparency and credibility of Fiji’s electoral process.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Education officer faces action over unapproved church group visit to Natabua High School
item.description: The Ministry of Education has launched action against an education officer who allegedly facilitated an unapproved visit by a foreign church group to Natabua High School, breaching strict school access and student safety protocols. The incident came to light after a video circulated on social media showing an interaction between a member of the Steadfast Baptist Church Group and a student during t
item.content:encoded:
The Ministry of Education has launched action against an education officer who allegedly facilitated an unapproved visit by a foreign church group to Natabua High School, breaching strict school access and student safety protocols.

The incident came to light after a video circulated on social media showing an interaction between a member of the Steadfast Baptist Church Group and a student during the group’s visit on 16 July 2026.

In a response, Minister for Education, Aseri Radrodro directed the Permanent Secretary for Education, Navin Raj, to take necessary action against the education officer responsible for allowing the visit without proper Ministry approval.

The group consisted of members from Houston, Texas, in the United States, along with a US national based in the Philippines.

This morning, Minister Radrodro met with representatives from the United States Embassy, who confirmed they had “no prior knowledge” of or involvement in the group’s activities in Fiji.

Minister Radrodro used the opportunity to reaffirm the Ministry’s unwavering commitment to student safety and the integrity of school access procedures.

“Our schools must remain safe and secure learning environments for every child,” said Radrodro.

“The Ministry has clear approval processes in place to safeguard students, and these requirements must be observed at all times. No organisation or individual should conduct activities in schools without the Ministry’s approval.”

The Minister stressed that all Ministry officers and school administrations are expected to strictly follow approval protocols for any external visits or activities. Where photography, filming, or publication of student images is involved, parental consent must also be obtained.

An investigation is now underway to establish the full circumstances of the incident, determine whether Ministry policies were breached, and recommend appropriate administrative measures.

The Ministry has reminded all organisations and individuals that “prior written approval” is mandatory before any engagement with students on school premises.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: High Court rules FICAC violated Charlie Charters’ constitutional rights
item.description: The High Court in Suva has permanently stayed the criminal proceedings against journalist Charlie Charters, ruling that the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC) violated his constitutional rights and abused the court process. In his ruling this morning, Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu found that Charters’ “right to remain silent i.e. no self-incrimination under Section 13 of the Cons
item.content:encoded:
The High Court in Suva has permanently stayed the criminal proceedings against journalist Charlie Charters, ruling that the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC) violated his constitutional rights and abused the court process.

In his ruling this morning, Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu found that Charters’ “right to remain silent i.e. no self-incrimination under Section 13 of the Constitution 2013 was violated by FICAC.”

The Judge also ruled that Charters’ “right to freedom of speech, expression, thought, opinion and publication under Section 17 of the Constitution 2013 including not to divulge the sources of his journalistic information was violated by FICAC.”

Justice Bulamainaivalu further found that “FICAC’s case on Counts 1–2 of aiding and abetting against the Applicant will inevitably or [is] doomed to fail.”

In relation to the conduct of the FICAC prosecution, the Judge stated that the court was “satisfied on the balance of probabilities that there was abuse of process by FICAC that fundamentally goes to the root of the trial of such a nature that nothing a trial magistrate can do in the conduct of the trial can relieve against its unfair consequences and an affront to the court’s conscience.”

Justice Bulamainaivalu also found that “there is a conflict of interest with regard to Count 2.”

He noted that the de facto officer doctrine barred the court from considering the validity of the Acting FICAC Commissioner’s appointment and alleged ultra vires acts, saying those issues are properly determined through judicial review proceedings.

Charters had been charged with two counts of aiding and abetting a FICAC officer to unlawfully divulge FICAC information under Section 13G of the FICAC Act.

FICAC alleged that between November 2 and December 14, 2025, and in February this year, Charters intentionally aided and abetted a FICAC officer by publishing official information relating to the functions of the Commission on his Facebook page without the permission of the FICAC Commissioner.

The matter had been before Magistrate Shageeth Somaratne.

Charters was represented by lawyers Juleen Fatiaki and Seforan Fatiaki.

Justice Bulamainaivalu made no order as to costs and granted FICAC 30 days to appeal to the Fiji Court of Appeal.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: High Court halts FICAC case against Charters
item.description: The High Court in Suva has permanently stayed the criminal proceedings brought against Charlie Charters by the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC). Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu delivered the ruling this morning.
item.content:encoded:
The High Court in Suva has permanently stayed the criminal proceedings brought against Charlie Charters by the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC).

Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu delivered the ruling this morning.

Mr Charters was charged with two counts of aiding and abetting a FICAC officer to unlawfully divulge FICAC information under Section 13G of the FICAC Act.

FICAC alleges that between November 2 and December 14, 2025, and February this year, Mr Charters intentionally aided and abetted a person, who was at the material time an officer of the commission, to commit an offence by publishing official information relating to the functions of the commission on his Facebook page without permission of the FICAC Commissioner.

His case was presided by Magistrate Shageeth Somaratne.

Mr Chaters is represented by Lawyers Juleen Fatiaki and Seforan Fatiaki

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Australian tourist pleads guilty in fatal crash that killed Fijian in Auckland
item.description: An Australian man has pleaded guilty to reduced charges over the fatal crash that claimed the life of Fijian Arvind “Ronald” Kumar near Auckland Airport last year. According to the New Zealand Herald , 37-year-old Melbourne resident Michael Rockafella admitted guilt to lesser charges after originally being charged with manslaughter.
item.content:encoded:
An Australian man has pleaded guilty to reduced charges over the fatal crash that claimed the life of Fijian Arvind “Ronald” Kumar near Auckland Airport last year.

According to the New Zealand Herald , 37-year-old Melbourne resident Michael Rockafella admitted guilt to lesser charges after originally being charged with manslaughter.

Mr Kumar, 45, was driving his wife to Auckland Airport at about 4am on July 30 last year so she could travel to Fiji to attend her aunt’s funeral when the crash occurred.

Court documents released publicly for the first time reveal Rockafella had spent the early hours of the morning at SkyCity Casino before driving a rental ute towards his airport hotel at high speed.

Authorities say he was about eight times over the high-risk threshold for methamphetamine intoxication, almost twice the legal alcohol limit, and also had traces of cannabis and a sedative in his system.

Police said Rockafella initially told investigators he had consumed only two whiskey and cola drinks.

CCTV footage showed he had already run a red light shortly after arriving in Auckland. After leaving the casino at 3.39am, his driving became increasingly dangerous.

The New Zealand Herald reports he was clocked travelling at 142km/h in a 100km/h zone on State Highway 20 and was seen weaving aggressively through traffic, including forcing his vehicle into a narrow gap between a moving car and a light truck.

When traffic was diverted from a closed section of the motorway, Rockafella allegedly continued driving erratically, nearly colliding with another vehicle and travelling in a bicycle lane before the fatal crash.

Read Today’s Fiji Times Read Todays Fiji Times Today's Paper Subscribe Trending Stories Grower urges military deployment to save crushing season Investigation nears end $650m debt write-off defended The walk for hope Former inmates urged to return to villages © 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Twelve workers lose jobs as court orders closure of South Seas Club
item.description: Twelve employees of Lautoka’s South Seas Club are jobless after the popular venue was abruptly closed under a court order, leaving workers locked out and uncertain about their jobs. The court order, executed on Monday, handed management of the club to an interim committee. When staff reported for work the following morning, they found the gates locked and a notice stating the club was “under the a
item.content:encoded:
Twelve employees of Lautoka’s South Seas Club are jobless after the popular venue was abruptly closed under a court order, leaving workers locked out and uncertain about their jobs.

The court order, executed on Monday, handed management of the club to an interim committee. When staff reported for work the following morning, they found the gates locked and a notice stating the club was “under the administrators of South Seas Club” and temporarily closed until further notice.

Employee Adi Laisa Volatabu said staff had received no communication from their employer and did not know where to turn for help.

Sera Tukana said workers were denied entry and instructed to surrender all club keys.

“The least they could do was give us notice and time to look for work somewhere else,” she said.

The closure has also affected tenants. Restaurant owner Hafiz Rahman said he was forced to shut a business he had operated at the club for more than 20 years.

“It came as a real shock and as a tenant I wished that I could have been told before this was going to happen,” he said.

Staff member Salote Ratu said three customers had already paid to hire the club’s hall, including one who had paid $1000 in full.

Former manager Jan Mohammed who’s in Japan said he would make an official statement after consulting with his lawyer.

The South Seas Club was ordered to be handed over to an interim committee in 2022, but the transfer was delayed while an appeal was before the Court of Appeal. Last week, the appeal was dismissed, allowing the committee led by Sanjay Prasad, Cecil James and Raymond Singh to assume control.

Lawyer Anil Prasad said his clients were carrying out the court’s orders. Asked about the workers, he said the court order had to be executed.

The affected employees have since approached the Ministry of Employment for advice on their rights and entitlements.

Employment Minister Agni Deo Singh said labour officers at the Lautoka officer were looking into the matter.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Government commits to dialogue over Momi Bay land dispute
item.description: The Coalition Government has pledged to pursue dialogue to resolve the long-running Momi Bay land issue, saying it is committed to balancing the concerns of traditional landowners with national development interests. In a statement, Minister for Lands Filimoni Vosarogo acknowledged the concerns raised by traditional landowners over the 2005 land swap associated with the Momi Bay Marriott Hotel dev
item.content:encoded:
The Coalition Government has pledged to pursue dialogue to resolve the long-running Momi Bay land issue, saying it is committed to balancing the concerns of traditional landowners with national development interests.

In a statement, Minister for Lands Filimoni Vosarogo acknowledged the concerns raised by traditional landowners over the 2005 land swap associated with the Momi Bay Marriott Hotel development.

He said the Government inherited a complex issue and was committed to addressing it “responsibly and in good faith.”

“Our Coalition Government has inherited this complex issue and is determined to approach it with fairness, transparency, and respect for all parties involved,” Mr Vosarogo said.

The Minister noted that while the land holds deep cultural and traditional significance for landowners, the development also carries significant commercial implications because of the Fiji National Provident Fund’s investment and its contribution to the country’s tourism industry.

He said the 2013 Constitution had created a legal framework that prevented simply reversing the land swap, making dialogue the most practical path forward.

“As a government, we are committed to finding the right balance. We will not ignore the legitimate concerns of our traditional landowners, nor will we undermine important commercial investments that benefit all Fijians, including through the FNPF,” Mr Vosarogo said.

The Minister called on all key stakeholders to work together toward an amicable resolution.

“We urge all parties — the traditional landowners, the iTaukei Land Trust Board (TLTB), and the FNPF — to engage in constructive and inclusive dialogue. We will facilitate good-faith discussions aimed at an amicable resolution that honors customary rights, addresses historical grievances where possible, and ensures the sustainable development of Momi Bay for the benefit of current and future generations.”

Mr Vosarogo said the Government believes open communication and mutual respect are essential to resolving the longstanding dispute and confirmed that the Ministry of Lands will coordinate discussions with all relevant stakeholders.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Shore Buses condemns stone-throwing incident
item.description: Shore Buses has condemned a stone-throwing incident involving one of its buses near the Nadonumai Roundabout, warning that such behaviour could have resulted in serious injury or loss of life. In a statement posted on social media, the company said the incident occurred at around 7:00pm on Thursday, when a group of young boys allegedly threw stones at a bus travelling through the area.
item.content:encoded:
Shore Buses has condemned a stone-throwing incident involving one of its buses near the Nadonumai Roundabout, warning that such behaviour could have resulted in serious injury or loss of life.

In a statement posted on social media, the company said the incident occurred at around 7:00pm on Thursday, when a group of young boys allegedly threw stones at a bus travelling through the area.

The company said the stones narrowly missed the driver and, fortunately, no passengers or staff were injured.

“By God’s grace, no passengers or our bus driver were injured,” the statement said.

Shore Buses believes the attacks may be linked to some youths becoming upset after being refused travel because they did not pay the required fare.

“This type of behaviour is believed to be happening because some youths become upset when they are not allowed to board the bus without paying their bus fare.”

The company stressed that all passengers are required to pay the fare, noting that the adult fare from Nadonumai to Suva is $1.25.

“Let us be clear: everyone must pay the required fare to travel.”

Shore Buses appealed directly to young people in the area to stop targeting public transport.

“To the young youths of Nadonumai, please STOP stoning buses. This is extremely dangerous and could seriously injure or even kill someone.”

The company also urged those responsible to consider the potential consequences of their actions.

“What if your mother, father, grandparents, brothers, sisters or friends were inside that bus and those stones hit them? Would you want that to happen?”

Shore Buses has called on parents, guardians, community leaders and residents to speak with young people about the dangers of such behaviour, saying attacks on buses place innocent lives at risk and damage public property.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
